source: CWE Top-25 / CERT-C / MISRA-C inspired rules for kernel and firmware code review
notes: Each rule requires concrete syntactic evidence on the flagged added line. Negative patterns exclude comments, string literals, and log calls. Aggregate checks require same-file scope evidence.

rules.description: Unsafe string/memory copy without explicit length bound
rules.risk: Unbounded copy can overflow destination buffer; use strlcpy/strlcat/snprintf with explicit size.
rules.action: Replace with length-bounded variant (strlcpy, strlcat, snprintf) and verify destination size.

rules.description: Pointer used after free in same changed hunk
rules.risk: Freeing a pointer that may still be referenced causes use-after-free.
rules.action: Set pointer to NULL after free; verify no remaining references before freeing.

rules.description: Pointer dereference via -> without preceding NULL check (excludes UEFI protocol globals)
rules.risk: Dereferencing a potentially NULL pointer causes kernel panic or undefined behavior.
rules.action: Add NULL check before dereference; use IS_ERR_OR_NULL() for error-pointer paths.

rules.description: Arithmetic on user-controlled or size values without overflow check
rules.risk: Integer overflow in size arithmetic can lead to under-allocation and heap overflow.
rules.action: Use checked arithmetic (check_add_overflow, check_mul_overflow) or saturating helpers.

rules.description: Allocation in changed lines without visible free path in same diff
rules.risk: Allocation without matching free on all error/exit paths causes memory leak.
rules.action: Ensure every allocation has a matching free on all exit paths; prefer devm_ variants for device-managed resources.

rules.description: Same pointer freed more than once in changed lines
rules.risk: Double-free corrupts heap allocator metadata and can lead to arbitrary code execution.
rules.action: Set pointer to NULL after first free; use a single canonical free path.

rules.description: Lock acquired in changed lines without matching unlock in same diff
rules.risk: Unbalanced lock acquisition causes deadlock or lock held across unexpected paths.
rules.action: Ensure every lock acquisition has a matching unlock on all exit paths; prefer scoped lock helpers.

rules.risk: Unchecked user-copy return value allows partial copy to go undetected, causing data corruption or info leak.
rules.action: Always check return value of copy_from/to_user; treat non-zero as error and propagate -EFAULT.

rules.description: Non-literal format string passed to printf-family function
rules.risk: Variable format string allows attacker-controlled format specifiers leading to info leak or memory corruption.
rules.action: Use a string literal as the format argument; pass variable data as subsequent arguments.

rules.description: Division or modulo where divisor may be zero
rules.risk: Division by zero causes kernel oops or undefined behavior.
rules.action: Guard divisor with explicit zero check before division; use DIV_ROUND_UP/DIV_ROUND_DOWN helpers.

rules.description: Reference count incremented without matching decrement in changed lines
rules.risk: Unbalanced refcount increment causes object to never be freed (memory/resource leak).
rules.action: Ensure every refcount_inc/kref_get has a matching put/dec on all exit paths.

rules.description: Return value of fallible kernel function discarded
rules.risk: Discarding return value of fallible function means errors go undetected, causing NULL dereference or resource leak.
rules.action: Assign return value and check for NULL/error before use.

rules.description: List/tree structure modified without lock in changed lines
rules.risk: Modifying shared list/tree without holding appropriate lock causes data structure corruption under concurrency.
rules.action: Hold the appropriate lock (spin_lock, mutex_lock, rcu_read_lock) around list/tree modifications.

rules.description: Hardcoded credential or secret literal in source
rules.risk: Hardcoded credentials in source are exposed in version control and binaries.
rules.action: Remove hardcoded credential; load from secure configuration, environment variable, or key management system.
